text: Hello everyone! I was discovering how documentation for a programming language could look like - I came with my own engine for Я where you can click on every token in code snippets and follow it. As a demonstration, there are first 6 chapters on polished step-by-step tutorials demonstrating designing a command line task manager: <https://muratkasimov.art/Ya/Tutorials/>. And here is an example with deconstructing an regular operator: <https://muratkasimov.art/Ya/Operators/kyokl/>.

text:
Hey folks, this is what I have been working on for past few months: <https://qwikbuild.com|https://qwikbuild.com>

The way I like to explain this to technical folks is: it is an AI coding agent not for you but for your friends and family members who likely are mobile-native, and tend to have much higher expectations from AI when it comes to building ready-to-use and full-featured web apps. :grinning:

Feedback and brickbats are welcome! :pray:

attachments.fallback: Qwikbuild AI - Build your App in 7 minutes on WhatsApp
attachments.text: Turn your creative ideas into professional websites and apps instantly. Send a voice note, screenshot, or example via WhatsApp, and our AI builds your site in minutes with stunning precision. No coding required.
attachments.title: Qwikbuild AI - Build your App in 7 minutes on WhatsApp

elements.text: Hey folks, this is what I have been working on for past few months:
https://qwikbuild.com

The way I like to explain this to technical folks is: it is an AI coding agent not for you but for your friends and family members who likely are mobile-native, and tend to have much higher expectations from AI when it comes to building ready-to-use and full-featured web apps.
Feedback and brickbats are welcome!

text:
First, congrats on launching this. Democratizing web dev is a laudable goal.

Second, this is such a polished product offering that I'm not sure how to offer feedback on it in this venue. I'm the wrong audience.

Is there any technical writing or behind-the-scenes material that might be a good fit for the folks in this community?

text:
Oh that's a really nifty idea! It makes exploring the code feel like falling down the Wikipedia rabbit hole.

I'm curious — the description of Я mentioned that there are elements of visual programming embedded in the text interface. Can you share a bit about that?

elements.text:
Oh that's a really nifty idea! It makes exploring the code feel like falling down the Wikipedia rabbit hole.

I'm curious — the description of Я mentioned that there are elements of visual programming
embedded in the text interface
Can you share a bit about that?

text: I see various pill-shaped symbols, but I'm not sure what they mean. (I'm only very lightly familiar with category theory.)

elements.text: I see various pill-shaped symbols, but I'm not sure what they mean
(I'm only very lightly familiar with category theory

text: Like, what's with the "stretched" versions of operators?

elements.text: Like, what's with the "stretched" versions of
operators?

text:
I haven't written any behind-the-scenes material for this yet, but this is free to try for everyone.
I was hoping people here might be able to try this and educate me about what the next critical pieces to build should be, to actually empower users who have been left out because of traditional ways of programming things.
It's a startup I'm building so I need to quickly learn what the actual user pains are.

text: About visual programming - code you see on the site you can see in Visual Studio Code, you just need to install a special font, this is how it looks like: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OJ6vb0mIiCU&list=PLzkSCNTBZnGj4KVpDHquWtf19e1ElvlbV&index=2>

elements.text: About visual programming - code you see on the site you can see in Visual Studio Code, you just need to install a special font, this is how it looks like:

text: About pill-shaped symbols - each letter means something (like Y stands for Yoneda, L for limits, etc). I actually even generate most of those operators in codebase since composition of functors is a very easy thing.

elements.text: About pill-shaped symbols - each letter means something (like Y stands for Yoneda, L for limits, etc). I actually even generate most of those operators in codebase since composition of functors is a very easy thing.

text: About stretched operators - to each operator name you can add underscore symbol (`_` ) to make it visually wider - it decreases precedence so that you don't even need parentheses to visually understand what is the order of operations.

elements.text: About stretched operators - to each operator name you can add underscore symbol (
) to make it visually wider - it decreases precedence so that you don't even need parentheses to visually understand what is the order of operations.
That's super clever

text:
Yeah, this project is fantastic. I've shared it internally at Ink & Switch since I know a few folks there will find it interesting, and I added it to my Visual Programming Codex. I hope you'll continue to share more about it here in this slack.

What's the current status of the project? I see lots of activity on Github, which is heartening.
